2005-01-01.title: As Officials Falter, the New Rich Roll in to Help
2005-01-01.abstract: India and Sri Lanka have promised to spare no expense to help tens of thousands whose lives were overturned in tsunami disaster; Sri Lanka is welcoming international aid, but India says it needs no outside help; newly wealthy, in region with history of natural calamity but uneven civic response, have played new role, raising money and delivering aid where wealthy Western countries once did; efforts have frequently been poorly focused, uncoordinated and blunted in some cases by distrust among government officials; situation in Nagappattinam, India, port town where 2,000 people died, noted; map; photos (M)

2005-01-01.title: Sudan and Southern Rebels Sign Pact to End Civil War
2005-01-01.abstract: Sudan government signs preliminary peace accord with Sudan People's Liberation Army that could end one of Africa's longest-running civil wars, even as conflict in Darfur continues; southern rebels took up arms in 1983, saying oil revenue was benefiting only north; rebel leader John Garang will become one of Pres Omar Hassan Ahmad al-Bashir's vice presidents under agreement; photos (M)

2005-01-01.title: Letter from Syria: Minority activists see beacon in a new Iraq
U.S. Vows Big Increase in Aid for Victims of Asian Disaster
2005-01-01.abstract: Pres Bush will increase emergency aid to stricken areas of Asia to $350 million from $35 million; says US will probably add more resources as scope of disaster becomes clearer; UN emergency aid coordinator Jan Egeland says new US money has increased overall amount donated to nearly $1.2 billion from 40 nations; Bush's increase in amount of aid comes after criticism that he had reacted too slowly to disaster (M)

2005-01-01.title: 3 Palestinians Are Killed in Gaza; Candidate Pays a Visit
2005-01-01.abstract: Three Palestinians are killed in Gaza on Dec 31, bringing Palestinian death toll to 12 during latest Israeli military operation to forestall rocket and mortar fire against 21 Israeli settlements; Mahmoud Abbas, leading candidate in Jan 9 presidential election, campaigns in Gaza (M)

2005-01-02.title: Israel Raids Gaza, a Week Before Palestinians Vote
Aid Summit Talks in Jakarta: U.S. Is Facing a Choice and an Opportunity
2005-01-02.abstract: The announcement by Asian leaders that they will hold a summit meeting in Jakarta on Thursday to coordinate aid for victims of the tsunami presents the Bush administration with the first of many choices about whether to seize on a disaster to change the nature of how President Bush approaches Asia in his second term.    Until now, the region has fit into Mr. Bush's grand strategy primarily as a second front in his battle against terrorism, and as a place where future conflicts -- with North Korea, between China and Taiwan or India and Pakistan -- must be defused. But beyond that, his agenda has been modest, at best.

2005-01-02.title: Tsunami Group Will Expand Its Network
2005-01-02.abstract: Officials in charge of the existing international tsunami warning system, which covers only the Pacific Ocean, have taken an initial step to broaden the network to the Indian Ocean and other possible trouble spots, agreeing to distribute their bulletins on earthquakes and possible waves ''to anyone who wants to receive the messages.''     Until now the bulletins had gone only to about 300 agencies and individual scientists tracking conditions mainly in the Pacific, which has historically experienced 90 percent of the world's underwater earthquakes and tsunamis.

2005-01-02.title: With $2 Billion Donated, U.N. Now Needs Help to Deliver Aid
2005-01-02.abstract: Jan Egeland, the United Nations' emergency relief coordinator, said Saturday that the commitment of relief money from more than 40 nations had reached $2 billion, but he said that the scale of the response was overwhelming the capacity to deliver aid.    ''The compassion has never ever been like this,'' he said, but then added, ''The military and civil defense assets that many countries are providing us are as valuable as cash or gold would be today because it makes us move with the assistance and it makes us get there in the race against the clock.''

2005-01-02.title: Armed and Elusive, Afghan Drug Dealers Roam Free
2005-01-02.abstract: Seen from the air, the Margo desert, which sprawls across the far southwestern corner of Afghanistan toward the borders with Iran and Pakistan, is traced with white car tracks.    With its forbidding reputation as the ''desert of death,'' it deters most travelers but is the favored route of drug traffickers taking opium, heroin and hashish produced in Afghanistan to Iran for smuggling to Turkey and Europe. They cross in armed convoys of 10 to 20 pickup trucks, at such high speed that police officials say they cannot catch them.

2005-01-03.title: Russia Turns Sour on North Korean Refugees
2005-01-03.abstract: Series of murky episodes, including deaths of four North Korean diplomats, have reinforcd sense that North Koreans are less than welcome in Vladivostok, Russia, where 2,500 of them are construction workers; in mid-1990's, Russian authorities were relatively lax about North Korean workers who managed to flee Siberian logging camps; fear now is that collapse of North Korea would bring uncontrolled flood of Korean refugees to region; map (M)

2005-01-03.title: A Village Grows Rich Off Its Main Export: Its Daughters
2005-01-03.abstract: Families in many small hamlets in China rely for their prosperity on daughters who go to Thailand and Malaysia to work in brothels; experts say that in some villages, majority of women in their 20's work in this trade, leaving almost no family untouched and young men without mates; photos (M)

2005-01-03.title: Kurds seek to maintain a fragile autonomy
Candidate Asks Gaza Militants to Stop 'Useless' Attacks on Israelis
2005-01-03.abstract: Mahmoud Abbas, favorite in impending Palestinian presidential election, visits northern Gaza Strip; calls on militants to stop firing rockets at Israel just hours after latest salvos prompted brief Israeli raid into area; he criticizes both Palestinian rocket fire and Israeli raid; tells militants such attacks are counterproductive (M)

2005-01-03.title: Names of the Dead
2005-01-03.abstract: Defense Dept confirms death of service member in Iraq (S)

2005-01-04.title: For British tabloids, no villain like the EU : Truth, or another media Euromyth?
Ex-Presidents Clinton and Bush to lead major fund-raising push to aid victims : U.S. bolsters call for disaster relief
Iraqi Premier Calls Bush to Discuss Obstacles to Election
2005-01-04.abstract: Prime Min Ayad Allawi telephones Pres Bush and discusses many impediments still facing Iraq as it heads toward elections in 27 days; makes call hours after wave of bombing attacks leaves at least 20 people dead; American officials say Allawi did not say elections should be delayed, but some officials in Washington and in Iraq interpret telephone call as sign that he may be preparing ground to make case for delay to Bush; Allawi is concerned his own party could be headed to defeat if election is held on schedule; his cabinet is already showing signs of weakening on question of holding elections this month; his defense minister Hazem Shaalan has suggested that postponement would encourage Sunnis to participate; American Embassy official says US wants elections to proceed as scheduled; photos (M)

2005-01-04.title: U.S. May Add Advisers to Aid Iraq's Military
2005-01-04.abstract: American military is considering adding hundreds of military advisers to work directly with Iraqi units, whose disappointing performance could jeopardize long-term American exit strategy from Iraq; new advisers would bolster Iraqi will to fight, help train officers who would lead troops, curb desertion and provide Iraqi forces with confidence that American units would back them up, in some cases fighting alongside them if needed; new advisers would join several hundred American troops already embedded with Iraqi units; embedding more Americans with Iraqis would mean diverting troops away from combat operations, possibly risky move at time when commanders say they need troops to press offensive against insurgents; but plan addresses widely acknowledged need; Pres Bush himself voiced concern about poor performance of Iraqi troops in Dec 20 news conference (M)

2005-01-05.title: Aboriginals Surviving in Highlands
2005-01-05.abstract: Aboriginal tribes on Andaman and Nicobar Islands in Bay of Bengal largely survived tsunami that devastated coastal areas of archipelago; isolation of six small tribes in highlands may have protected them; Indian government is bringing in aid but continues to bar foreign relief, citing need to protect indigenous tribes; aid groups seek local partners to get around ban; nearly 7,000 people are confirmed dead out of 350,000 on 36 inhabited islands (M)

2005-01-05.title: Giving for a Cause, and That Cause Only
2005-01-05.abstract: Donors to disaster relief effort in south Asia are often specifying that gifts go exclusively to help those victims (S)

2005-01-05.title: Powell views devastation in hard-hit Indonesian province
Korea's Tricky Task: Digging Up Past Treachery
2005-01-05.abstract: South Korea's political and intellectual class turns to looking back, both at military era that ended in 1980's and at Japanese colonial period that ended six decades ago; photos; examinations raise sensitive issues about collaboration with occupiers and, more recently, about responsibility for political assassinations and antidemocratic crackdowns (M)

2005-01-05.title: To Those Seeking Help and Giving It, Computer Is a Lifeline
2005-01-05.abstract: Americans turn to Internet for word of people who might have fallen victim to tsunami in southern Asia, posting descriptions and mesages on dozens of sites; Internet has also become conduit for big donations to relief organizations (S)

2005-01-06.title: Iraqi leader says vote will be held
Officials gather in Jakarta for tsunami aid talks
World Briefing | Americas: Chile: Pinochet Under House Arrest
2005-01-06.abstract: General Augusto Pinochet is officially under house arrest at his estate west of Santiago, Chile; is to stand trial for murder and kidnapping that occurred during his 17-year dictatorship; photo (S)

2005-01-06.title: Land Rush by Foreign Investors Divides South Africa
2005-01-06.abstract: Breathtaking coastline of South Africa's Eastern Cape, hidden from world for decades under apartheid, has become object of minor land rush by foreign investors; this has touched off emotional debate in country about whether to welcome investors or fend them off with new restrictions on foreign land ownership; government panel formed to investigate issue will deliver interim report this month; nation's blacks, forbidden to own land during apartheid, see hotter property market mainly benefiting South African whites, who could amass property under apartheid; photo; map (M)

2005-01-07.title: Uncounted Costs: Legions of Orphans and Broken Hearts
2005-01-07.abstract: Tsunami left thousands of orphans in its wake in Aceh Province, Indonesia, although numbers of orphans are low because number of children who died was high; rarely was child able to survive where adults could not; photo (M)

2005-01-07.title: Four areas of Iraq too unsafe for voting
Europa: In Poland, solidarity with Ukraine's struggle
No 'Smoking Gun' in the Inquiry Into Iraq's Prewar Oil Sales
2005-01-07.abstract: Paul A Volcker says report of his investigation into scandal-tainted oil-for-food program in Iraq will not produce any 'smoking gun'; appears to be seeking to lower expectations of what might be in his much-anticipated preliminary report, due this month; program's former director Benon V Sevan has been accused of accepting illegal oil allocations worth millions of dollars, and United Nations Secretary General Kofi Annan has come under personal suspicion because one of contracting companies employed his son Kojo Annan; Volcker's final report is due in June (M)

2005-01-07.title: World Briefing | Middle East: Egypt: CT Scan May Solve Tut Mystery
2005-01-07.abstract: Dr Zahi Hawass, Egypt's chief archaeologist, announces removal of King Tut's mummy from tomb at Luxor so scientists can perform three-dimensional CT scan of bones; it is unknown how 17-year-old King Tut died and scans may provide answers about his death; photo (S)

2005-01-08.title: U.S. weighs options on non-Iraqi captives
No sign of missing French journalist
Chirac hints he'll run again for president
Chirac hints he'll run again for president

2005-01-09.title: Echo of Gdansk Heard in Ukraine's Shout for Democracy
2005-01-09.abstract: Veterans of Solidarity movement in Gdansk, Poland, 25 years ago see echoes of their struggle in Ukraine's orange revolution; Solidarity was only self-governing, legally recognized labor union in Soviet bloc in 1981, and thus stakes were higher for pioneering Poles; Ukrainian struggle for democracy enjoys almost automatic support from West, while Western Europe was cautious of Solidarity disturbing detente, although US Pres Reagan supported it; most important similarity is effort to avoid provoking violence; map; photos (M)

2005-01-09.title: Sudan Government and Rebels in Deal to End Fighting in South
U.S. Bombs Wrong House, Killing at Least Five Iraqis
2005-01-09.abstract: F-16 fighter mistakenly drops 500-pound bomb on wrong house during attack on insurgents in village of Aitha in northern Iraq, killing at least five Iraqis; owner of house says 14 people, all from same family, died; photo (M)

2005-01-09.title: Tsunami Tests U.S. Forces' Logistics, but Gives Pentagon a Chance to Show a Human Face
2005-01-09.abstract: Defense Dept carefully balanced its strategic needs with imperative to open up logistical bottlenecks and begin ferrying water, food, medical supplies and shelter for relief of tsunami victims; has calculated how much combat power it needed to reserve in Pacific to maintain credible deterrent against North Korea and China while sending assistance; Brig Gen Jan-Marc Jouas hopes operation will show world generosity of American people; photo (M)

2005-01-09.title: Forensic Teams in Thailand Fight Against Heat and Time
2005-01-09.abstract: Teams of forensic technicians from 30 countries are working day and night to collect DNA to identify dead from tsunami; Thailand has promised to respect wishes of Western governments to repatriate all identifiable remains of foreigners and none have been cremated; tsunami is believed to have killed hundreds of undocumented workers from Myanmar, complicating matters; map, photos (M)

2005-01-10.title: Indonesian City Gets MoreHelp: $3.30-a-Day Jobs
2005-01-10.abstract: American-based private relief agency Mercy Corps starts Cash for Work program in one neighborhood in Banda Aceh, Indonesia, offering men who have lost their homes and livelihoods paid jobs, clearing debris and removing bodies; men clearing debris are paid standard wage for unskilled labor there; those collecting bodies are paid more; it took five days of negotiations before government would sign off on Mercy Corps work program; elsewhere in city, cleanup is being organized by Indonesian military; with shortage of earth-moving equipment and vastness of Aceh Province, job could take months, if not years; this makes Mercy Corps work program useful addition; Mercy Corps hopes to expand program to have 1,000 people in project and expand scope of work to include helping out at local schools; photos (M)

2005-01-10.title: Report From Panel Shows Lapses In U.N.'s Oil-for-Food Program
2005-01-10.abstract: Independent commission investigating United Nations oil-for-food program in Iraq says UN's auditors did not monitor giant aid effort adequately and that its officials in some cases ignored recommendations that auditors deemed crucial to program's success; commission, lead by Paul A Volcker, issues what it terms 'provisional assessment of auditor performance; release of confidential documents shows loose financial controls over sprawling program, which has become major scandal at UN; there are no allegations of bribery or corruption by UN officials, but audits make clear that many of deficiencies were known in late 1990's, at time when indications of corruption of program by Saddam Hussein and others were also reaching UN; commission notes auditors' failure to monitor in depth New York headquarters of office that administered program, or to monitor contracts for oil sales that provided revenue for program or those for purchase of relief and other goods supposed to ease effect of sanctions on Iraqis; commission notes that program suffered from 'chronic shortage' of auditors (M)

2005-01-10.title: New premier is picked forSerb-run half of Bosnia
Pact ends one of Sudan's civil wars
But country won't be less secure, he asserts : Powell admits to concern over a postelection Iraq

2005-01-11.title: Italy's ban on smokinggets off to a fuming start
U.S. Agrees to Release Last 4 Britons From Guantanamo Prison
Bedlam in the Air for China Flight Attendants
2005-01-11.abstract: China has spent fortune assembling one of world's largest passenger airline fleets; it transported 80 million passengers last year, fourfold increase from 1991; front line workers in country's mass air-travel revolution are China's flight attendants, most of them women; they like job's middle class wages, but work shifts are brutal, and they must deal with intemperate passengers; there is little regulation of working conditions; one trouble-laden flight described; photo (M)

2005-01-11.title: Routine Helps Students Deal With Wave's Toll
2005-01-11.abstract: Along coast of southern Thailand, schools face challenge not only of rounding up displaced children and finding new quarters, but of helping pupils deal with emotional toll of devastating tsunami; some are orphans, and mmny more lost either mother or father; photo (M)

2005-01-12.title: World Briefing | Asia: Pakistan: Musharraf Bomber On The Run
2005-01-12.abstract: In a major security breach, a Pakistani man sentenced to death for trying to assassinate President Pervez Musharraf in 2003 has escaped from prison, officials disclosed, and a nationwide hunt is under way. The man, Mushtaq Ahmed, escaped from an air force jail in the northern city of Rawalpindi on Nov. 25 when he was allowed to take a bath and his guard fell asleep, according to an initial report. He had been sentenced to death in the Dec. 14, 2003, assassination attempt against General Musharraf -- the first of two attempts that month -- when a bridge blew up seconds after his motorcade passed by. No one was hurt.   Salman Masood (NYT)

2005-01-12.title: 4 Britons and an Australian to Be Freed at Guantánamo
2005-01-12.abstract: US agrees to release last four Britons and one Australian who have been held without charge at Guantanamo Bay Naval Base for around three years; US statement says Britain and Australia have accepted responsibility to prevent suspects from engaging in terrorist activities in future; Sec Jack Straw says British police will decide whether to arrest them again; photos (M)

2005-01-12.title: U.S. and Russia Seeking Limits on Portable Antiaircraft Missiles
2005-01-12.abstract: US and Russia are close to agreement on curbing trafficking in shoulder-fired antiaircraft missiles often used by terrorists; Min Sergei B Ivanov and Sec Donald Rumsfeld detail accord at news conference in Washington (M)

2005-01-12.title: $717 Million Pledged to U.N. Aid Effort
2005-01-12.abstract: Jan Egeland of UN says donor countries have pledged $717 million to UN appeal for emergency help for ten Asian countries hit by earthquake and tsunami (S)

2005-01-12.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Putin Vetoes Beer Ban
2005-01-12.abstract: President Vladimir V. Putin vetoed a new law restricting the sale and public consumption of beer. The restrictions were adopted by the upper and lower houses of Parliament last year as part of a legislative assault on beer, which until now has not been covered by laws regulating sales and advertising of wine and hard liquor and as a result has become ubiquitous on city streets across Russia. In a letter to lawmakers, Mr. Putin criticized the law for a lack of specifics and suggested that local governments should be empowered to draft their own restrictions on public places where beer drinking would be banned.   Steven Lee Myers (NYT)

2005-01-13.title: Jakarta says it wants all foreign troopsto leave in 3 months
World Briefing | Europe: Germany: 22 Islamic Suspects Held In Raids
2005-01-13.abstract: German police arrest 22 people suspected of being members of extremist Islamic network based in Ulm (S)

2005-01-13.title: Blair defends laws allowing pubs to stay open past 11
World Briefing | Americas: Chile: Bail For Pinochet
2005-01-13.abstract: Chilean appeals court approves $3,500 bail for 89-year-old former dictator Gen Augusto Pinochet, who has been under house arrest charged with kidnapping and murder stemming from his time as Chilean leader (S)

2005-01-13.title: Indonesia Orders Foreign Troops Providing Aid to Leave by March 26
2005-01-13.abstract: Indonesia announces that all foreign troops assisting in tsunami relief operation must leave by late March; government is sensitive to impression that it is relying too heavily on outside military forces; number of countries, including United States, have sent or are sending troops to help; Indonesia military has fought civil war against separatist rebels in Aceh Province for 30 years and has kept Aceh virtually sealed to outsiders in that period; photo (M)

2005-01-14.title: Sharon Cuts Israeli Ties With Palestinian Authority
U.S. Army to Expand Role in Iraq's Civil Engineering
2005-01-14.abstract: Project and Contracting Office, formed by Pentagon to oversee $18.4 billion program to rebuild Iraq's physical infrastructure, will begin handing its responsibilities over to United States Army Corps of Engineers over next several months; Charles Hess, director of office, denies that move is response to Iraqi and American criticism that reconstruction effort focused too much on large projects, started up too slowly and failed to anticipate attacks by insurgents; defends reconstruction program as successful, given hard realities of Iraq (M)

2005-01-14.title: Once a Village, Now Nothing: Even the Bodies Are Gone
2005-01-14.abstract: Indonesian coastal town of Calang, where thousands of people were swept away by tsunami, has not just been destroyed, it has vanished; most of town's 7.300 people are missing; only 323 bodies have been found; Calang and many other villages on western coast of Aceh Province have been wiped from map; suffering along land closest to earthquake's epicenter has been compounded by its remoteness; devastation is great and numbers in need is huge; much of terrain is accessible only by helicopter; photos; map (M)

2005-01-14.title: U.S. Calls Indonesia Deadline for Troop Pullout Reasonable
2005-01-14.abstract: American Amb B Lynn Pascoe says United States is not troubled by Indonesian government's demands that aid workers in Aceh Province register and that all foreign troops be gone by end of March; describes restrictions as 'reasonable'; Indonesian government has said its curbs on aid workers in Aceh, which has been torn by civil war for 30 years, are necessary for their protection; pohto (M)

2005-01-14.title: Re-election strategy also includes reform: Blair turns focus to prosperity

2005-01-15.title: World Briefing | Americas: Venezuela: Chávez Ends Accords With Colombia
2005-01-15.abstract: Pres Hugo Chavez of Venezuela suspends bilateral accords and commercial relations with Colombia until government in Bogota apologizes for its role in capture of rebel commander in Caracas (S)

2005-01-15.title: China Promotes Another Boom: Nuclear Power
2005-01-15.abstract: China, in its seemingly bottomless demand for electricity, plans to build nuclear power reactors on scale and pace comparable to most ambitious nuclear energy programs world has ever seen; current plans call for new reactors to be commissioned at rate of nearly two a year between 2005 and 2020; China's eight nuclear reactors supply less than 2 percent of nation's current demand; critics question whether such small payoff warrants exposure to risk of catastrophic failures, nuclear proliferation, terrorism and unresolved problems of radioactive waste disposal; map; photo shows control room of China's nuclear power plant in Daya Bay (M)

2005-01-15.title: Rich Nations Are Urged to Ease Trade With Affected Countries
2005-01-15.abstract: World Trade Organization dir gen Supachai Panitchpakdi issues plea to member nations to lower trade barriers as way of helping nations ravaged by tsunami (M)

2005-01-15.title: Wary Sri Lanka fishermen venture back to sea
Six-party talks may resume within weeks, U.S. says: N. Korea will return to nuclear discussions

2005-01-16.title: New U.S. Commander Sees Shift in Military Role in Iraq
2005-01-16.abstract: Lt Gen John R Vines, who will take over in mid-February as American ground commander in Iraq, says main job after elections will be to improve training of Iraqi security forces; as many as 10,000 American military advisors could be assigned to work directly with Iraqi units; Vines has assigned his commanders and senior officers reading list on Islam and brought in Islamic scholars to help them understand culture and region; photos (M)

2005-01-16.title: Thai Doctor Fashions a Life Working Among the Dead
2005-01-16.abstract: Profile of Dr Khunying Porntip Rojanasunan, deputy director of Thailand's Central Institute of Forensic Science; purple-haired former movie star has been directing post-tsunami forensic operation at Yan Yao temple, but she has been criticized for not following international procedures for identifying dead; Thai officials have ordered that 800 bodies sent to cemetery for burial be returned to temple morgue for further testing and to make sure no foreigners are among them; police will take over identification process and at least 2,000 unidentified bodies will be retested; map; photo (M)

2005-01-16.title: Titan's Big News: A Mysterious Shoreline
2005-01-16.abstract: European Space Agency spacecraft Huygens lands on Saturn's moon Titan and shows pictures of drainage channels running to mysterious shoreline; flat dark areas could be lakes of liquid methane or just flat surface of dark material; craft sends back data and images for one hour and ten minutes; effort is hampered by loss of one communication channel for transmitting data to Cassini mother ship, cutting number of pictures to 350 from planned 700, but mission is considered success; photo (M)

2005-01-16.title: Bush Signals Steadfast Approach to Iraq
Estonian Wind Farm Now Grows Where Soviet Guards Trained

2005-01-17.title: How Top Spies in Ukraine Changed the Nation's Path
2005-01-17.abstract: Article, Back Channels, recounts how Ukraine's intelligence services thwarted military crackdown against democracy demonstrators massed in Kiev last November; senior intelligence officials warned Interior Ministry that using force against peaceful rallies was illegal and said army and security services would defend civilians against ministry troops; exchanges among top officials were hidden factor in Orange Revolution that overturned fraudulent election and changed Ukraine's political course, and are rare in former Soviet states where security agencies are often most conservative, ruthless instruments of state power; inside battle was waged by clique of top officials, known as siloviki, who chose not to follow Pres Leonid Kuchma's plan to pass power to Prime Min Viktor Yanukovich; Oleksander Galaka, head of military intelligence, and Col Gen Ihor P Smeshko were among those warning Lt Gen Sergei Popkov not to send in Interior Ministry troops; officers also funneled information to opposition led by Viktor Yushchenko and engaged in psychological tug-of-war to soften government response to protests; group also included Lt Gen Lt Gen Oleksander Skibinetsky and Lt Gen Oleksander Skipalsky, who had relatives in crowds; photos; day-by-day developments recounted (L)

2005-01-17.title: Sharon Orders New Crackdown on Gaza Factions
2005-01-17.abstract: Prime Min Ariel Sharon, in blunt warning to new Palestinian leader, warns Mahmoud Abbas that Israeli military will act 'without restrictions' against Palestinians stepping up attacks in Gaza; PLO issues rare call for halt to 'all military acts' that provide 'excuses' to Israel, reflecting efforts of Abbas, who is also PLO chairman; Hamas renews rocket and mortar fire in Gaza; Israeli gunfire reportedly kills two Palestinians; Israeli troops pull back from outskirts of Gaza City after clashes in which eight Palestinians are killed, most of them militants; photo; Palestinian shelling wounds two Israeli children at Gaza settlement and teenage girl in Sederot (M)

2005-01-17.title: High-Ranking Officers May Face Prosecution in Iraqi Prisoner Abuse, Military Officials Say
2005-01-17.abstract: Lists of millions of registered Iraqi voters are being sent to election officials in Baghdad and will be posted at district offices around country; UN oficial Carlos Valenzuela says workers are close to settling on more than 5,000 polling places; election timetable; US forces continue raids in Mosul and Kirkuk, finding weapons caches and insurgents; photos; Valenzuela also reports eight election workers killed; denies mass resignations in Mosul and Anbar (M)

2005-01-17.title: China Curbs Discussion of Late Leader Who Opposed Crackdown
No Honeymoon for Palestinian Chief
2005-01-17.abstract: News analysis on immediate turmoil facing new Palestinian leader Mahmoud Abbas, as Prime Min Ariel Sharon lays down tough Israel markers and Palestinian militants attack Karni checkpoint, killing six Israeli civilians and wounding five others (M)

2005-01-18.title: World Briefing | Asia: Pakistan: Arrest In Musharraf Plot
2005-01-18.abstract: Pakistan authorities arrest Muhammad Jamil Memon, who has confessed to role in 2002 plot to assassinated Pres Pervez Musharraf and failed attack on Karachi's American Consulate (S)

2005-01-18.title: World Briefing | Europe: Ministers Say I.R.A. Robbed Belfast Bank
2005-01-18.abstract: Irish Foreign Min Dermot Ahern and Paul Murphy, Britain's secretary of state for Northern Ireland, hold that recent Belfast bank robbery, which they say was conducted by Irish Republican Army, has seriously compromised efforts to restore local government; robbery netted $34 million (S)

2005-01-18.title: Abbas Tells Palestinian Forces to Block Attacks on Israel
2005-01-18.abstract: Palestinian Authority's new president Mahmoud Abbas orders his security forces to try to prevent attacks against Israel; move could put him on collision course with armed Palestinian factions; he will not give details about how security forces might act against militant groups, and there is no sign that any action has started; Abbas has publicly opposed attacks on Israel as counterproductive, but genuine crackdown on Hamas, Islamic Jihad and other groups that have sworn to continue their attacks could put him in full-fledged confrontation with them; solid majority of Palestinians support suicide bombings and shootings to oppose Israeli occupation in West Bank and Gaza and to fight for statehood, although there are signs that support has declined as violence grinds on; photo (M)

2005-01-18.title: U.N. Panel Urges Doubling of Aid to Cut Poverty
2005-01-18.abstract: United Nations-sponsored international team says drastically reducing poverty in its many guises--hunger, illiteracy, disease--is 'utterly affordable' if industrial nations agree to double aid to poor countries, to one-half of 1 percent of national incomes, or 50 cents out of every $100 of incomes; report by so-called United Nations Millennium Project advocates reforms to ease trade barriers and sweeping investments in health, education, rural development, road building, housing and scientific research; blueprint wins quick praise from heads of International Monetary Fund and World Bank; some critics view its approach as utopian overreaching; others worry that it puts too little emphasis on need for poor countries to make deep political and social changes to reduce poverty; project's agenda is first in series this year intended to refocus attention on fulfilling promises to fight poverty that were made at UN in 2000; photos (M)

2005-01-19.title: Kidnappers free bishop after one day
World Briefing | Europe: Ukraine: Inauguration Clears A Hurdle
2005-01-19.abstract: Ukrainian Supreme Court lifts ban and allows newspapers to publish results of last month's presidential election; action clears way for inauguration of Viktor A Yushchenko, despite outstanding court ruling on challenge levied by former Prime Min Viktor F Yanukovich (S)

2005-01-19.title: Ex-Iran leader cool to Bush warning on arms
Archbishop Freed in Iraq, but 8 Chinese Are Captives
2005-01-19.abstract: Insurgents in Iraq release Syrian Catholic archbishop, Basile Georges Casmoussa, day after he was snatched from streets of Mosul; new hostage drama pens as eight Chinese construction workers are shown on video, held at gunpoint by masked men; interior minister Falah Hassan al-Naqib warns Iraq will tear itself apart unless enough people vote in election; Amb John Negroponte says elections can be successful, while admitting there is no end in sight to insurgency and US does not know how big it is; international group reports 18,000 Iraqi expatriates in 14 countries have registered to vote so far; US soldier is killed by bomb in Baghdad, second in western Iraq; three political candidates reported killed; photos of Iraqi girl screaming after parents are killed when US soldiers fire on car when it failed to stop, and of British soldier feigning punch at bound Iraqi prisoner (M)

2005-01-19.title: U.S. Intelligence Says Iraqis Will Press for Withdrawal
2005-01-19.abstract: New US intelligence estimates reportedly predict that post-election government in Iraq will almost certainly ask US to set specific timetable for withdrawing troops; reports, which assume Shiite election win, see even more violence, including Shiite-Sunni clashes that could lead to civil war; request would put new pressure on Bush administration to speed training of Iraqi forces able to confront insurgency; grim tone of new assessments comes as officials like Sec Colin Powell acknowledge that 'raging insurgency' is not likely to fade soon (M)

2005-01-20.title: Bombing spree in Iraq leaves up to 26 dead
Terror threat seen rising ahead of vote in Denmark
Israel and Palestinians to resume security contacts
Names of the Dead
2005-01-20.abstract: Defense Dept confirms deaths of two more Americans in Iraq, bringing total to 1,364 (S)

2005-01-20.title: Rice gets skeptical questions at hearing

2005-01-21.title: Britain Tries to Tone Down Debate Over Prisoner Abuse
2005-01-21.abstract: British military authorities and politicians seek to limit public debate about allegations that three British soldiers abused Iraqi prisoners shortly after they occupied Basra in 2003; there is widening revulsion in other European countries; men are being court-martialed in Germany; Prime Min Tony Blair moves swiftly to try to prevent scandal from adding to political costs of Britain's deployment in Iraq; he and his adversaries in Parliament seem united in ascribing abuses in Basra to tiny minority of British troops (M)

2005-01-21.title: British Major Denies Abuse of Iraqis
Iraqi Refugee's Tale of Abuse Dissolves Upon Later Scrutiny
2005-01-21.abstract: Washington Post publishes follow-up article questioning earlier article about claims by Jumana Michael Hanna, Iraqi woman, about her horrific torture at hands of Saddam Hussein's regime; Hanna's tale was unusual in that she was willing to name Iraqi police officials who participated in her alleged rape and torture over two-year period of imprisonment; she was granted refugee status by United States officials on basis of her claims; articles in Esquire magazine and Post now conclude that none of her allegations could be verified; apparent debunking of her story raises questions about her embrace by officials from Coalition Provisional Authority in 2003 (M)

2005-01-21.title: Yushchenko Set for Oath This Sunday
2005-01-21.abstract: Ukraine's Parliament votes to inaugurate Viktor A Yushchenko as country's next president on Jan 23, nearly three months after first votes were cast; inauguration will end electoral crisis that prompted huge public demonstrations and behind-the-scenes intrigue, deeply dividing country and souring its relations with Russia; Pres Vladimir V Putin, who had supported Yushchenko's opponent Viktor F Yanukovich, congratulates Yushchenko in statement passed through diplomatic channels, rather than telephone call; Yushchenko's upcoming trip to Moscow will be test of his ability to overcome bitterness caused by what many of his supporters saw as Russian interference in campaign; Yushchenko intends to tour Europe after his Russian visit, underscoring his efforts to balance relations between Russia and West (M)

2005-01-21.title: U.S. Vows to Attain Global Warning System
2005-01-21.abstract: United States pledges to expand emergency tsunami and earthquake warning system to cover globe; Japan promises to start immediately stitching together emergency warning system for Indian Ocean (M)

2005-01-22.title: ECB fight pits small against big
Mystery in Iraq as $300 Million is Taken Abroad
2005-01-22.abstract: Earlier this month, according to Iraqi officials, $300 million in American bills was taken out of Iraq's Central Bank, put into boxes and quietly put on a charter jet bound for Lebanon.    The money was to be used to buy tanks and other weapons from international arms dealers, the officials say, as part of an accelerated effort to assemble an armored division for the fledgling Iraqi Army. But exactly where the money went, and to whom, and for precisely what, remains a mystery, at least to Iraqis who say they have been trying to find out.

2005-01-22.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Beslan Protest Over Siege Inquiry
2005-01-22.abstract: Beslan residents block Rostov-Baku highway for second day to protest Russian Parliament commission's handling of inquiry into September 2004 school siege and call for North Ossetian President Aleksandr S Dzasokhov's resignation (S)

2005-01-22.title: Agent for Saddam Hussein Met With Carter and Kemp
2005-01-22.abstract: An Iraqi-American businessman who pleaded guilty this week to secretly lobbying influential Americans on behalf of Saddam Hussein pursued contacts with Democrats like former President Jimmy Carter and Republicans like Jack Kemp, the former vice presidential candidate, government officials said Friday.    The businessman, Samir A. Vincent, is now cooperating with a federal investigation into corruption in the United Nations oil-for-food aid program for Iraq. He pleaded guilty Tuesday to charges that he had pocketed millions in hidden oil profits in exchange for helping the Iraqi government in its efforts to end economic sanctions imposed in 1990.

2005-01-22.title: Road design? He calls it a revolution

2005-01-23.title: Israeli and Palestinian Officials Sound Hopeful Notes
Iraqis Abroad Get More Time to Register
Sleepy Mexican Border Towns Awake to Drug Violence
2005-01-23.abstract: Fighting among Mexico's powerful drug cartels in Nuevo Laredo has spawned sharp rise in killings and kidnappings; American officials warn that Mexican drug traffickers with false identification have taken up residence on US side of border; Americans in border cities have been kidnapped; many Mexicans do not report missing children because they do not trust Mexican authorities; former special forces officers known as Zetas are among most ruthless new players fighting for control of drug traffic through Laredo, Tex; map; photos (M)

2005-01-23.title: GENERAL SEEKING FASTER TRAINING OF IRAQ SOLDIERS
2005-01-23.abstract: Retired Gen Gary E Luck, sent to assess operations in Iraq, has concluded that American troops must speed up and strengthen training of Iraqi security forces by assigning as many as 8,000 to 10,000 additional military advisers to work directly with Iraqi units and tailor their assistance and partnership to array of Iraqi security forces; is expected to recommend that allied military officials fill several advisers' positions in interior and defense ministries; number of trained Iraqi personnel is disputed (M)

2005-01-23.title: Homes of Israeli Settlers Pose a New Set of Anxieties
2005-01-23.abstract: Israeli government and Palestinian Authority both say they want to avoid chaotic transfer when Israel leaves Gaza this summer; both sides for different reasons are seriously considering tearing down about 1,600 settlers' houses in Gaza; many Israelis cannot bear thought of Palestinians living in their houses; Palestinian Authority says houses do not really suit Palestinians' needs but could spark land rush; map; photo (M)

2005-01-24.title: In tsunami area, anger at evangelists
After Treating Victims' Bodies, Indonesia and Sri Lanka Turn to Hearts and Minds
2005-01-24.abstract: Four weeks after tsunami, attention begins to move from urgent physical needs of victims in Sri Lanka and Indonesia to survivors' mental health; neither nation has many psychologists or psychiatrists, but each is hoping to use foreign aid to train health workers and others to provide short-term support and to recognize the minority of people who are at risk for developing disorders like depression and post-traumatic stress; photo (M)

2005-01-24.title: U.S. to provide heavy security for Iraq voters
Beijing's Planning of Zhao's Funeral Is Slowed by Disputes

2005-01-25.title: Party's failure to honor Nazi victims is major embarrassment : Germany at a loss over walkout
Plan Offers Autonomy for Enclave in Georgia
2005-01-25.abstract: Pres Mikhail Saakashvili of Georgia releases details of proposal offering autonomy to South Ossetia, breakaway region along Georgia's northern border with Russia that has been all but independent since fighting civil war against Georgia in early 1990's; proposal would grant region new political rights and economic benefits; South Ossetia's president, Eduard D Kokoity, rejects proposal sight unseen; refers to Georgia as 'neighboring country' even though South Ossetia lies within Georgia's internationally recognized borders; photo (M)

2005-01-25.title: Bulgaria cracks down on television investigators
A Sunni Runs for Office and Maybe His Life
2005-01-25.abstract: Dr Riyadh N al-Adhadh, Sunni Arab, is running for Baghdad provincial council seat, even as powerful Sunni groups are calling for boycott of elections; his own party has withdrawn from election, and has not responded to his request for permission to take seat if elected; he says it is important to take part in process, and to make 'the occupier,' as he refers to Americans, repair all damage that has taken place in Iraq because of US invasion; photo (M)

2005-01-25.title: A.C.L.U. Presents Accusations of Serious Abuse of Iraqi Civilians
2005-01-25.abstract: American Civil Liberties Union releases documents describing complaints of serious abuse of Iraqi civilians, including reports of electric shocks and forced sodomy, and accuses military of not thoroughly investigating cases; documents list dozens of allegations of abuse at American detention centers that began at same time such acts were occurring at Abu Ghraib prison; it is not clear whether every case described is new incident; new accusations generally concern behavior of American Special Forces, as opposed to prison guards or interrogators accused at Abu Ghraib; documents were provided to ACLU as part of its suit (M)

2005-01-26.title: World Briefing | Europe: Germany: Vow To Remember Nazi Crimes
2005-01-26.abstract: German Chancellor Gerhard Schroder, speaking on eve of 60th anniversary of Auschwitz death camp liberation, calls for rememberance and perserverance to combat anti-semitism and neo-nazism (S)

2005-01-26.title: Paternity Suit Against TV Star Scandalizes Egyptians
2005-01-26.abstract: Egypt is scandalized by paternity suit brought by Hind el-Hinnawy, 27-year-old costume designer who says Ahmed el-Fishawy, famous 24-year-old actor is her daughter's father; their photos; unmarried upper-class young women in Egypt usually have abortions in such situations and then are married off to first unwitting suitor family can snare; Hinnawy touches off debate over Sunni practice of urfi marriages, clandestine private contracts that young people use to have sex; her lawyer seeks DNA test of Fishawy, who hosted television talk show dispensing advice to devout Muslim youth (M)

2005-01-26.title: In Thrown-to-Lions Trial, Bloody Clothes Remain
2005-01-26.abstract: Prosecutors in rural South African hamlet produced blood-soaked clothes of man they say was fed to lions by former employer; Mark Scott-Crossley allegedly ordered two other workers to beat Nelson Chisale and dump him in nearby breeding enclosure for 20 rare white lions (S)

2005-01-26.title: Corrections
2005-01-26.abstract: A headline on Monday with an article about the 1981 killing of a Cornell University student by a serial killer now on death row in Connecticut omitted attribution for the claim that the student's family was not informed until recently that the case was solved in 1987. That is the family's contention. Because of an editing error, the article referred imprecisely to the accounts given by the family of the victim, Dzung Ngoc Tu, and by Cornell. The sides differ on when and how the university informed the family about the investigation; their differences go beyond conflicting memories and lingering questions. Cornell officials disclosed records yesterday describing their contacts with the family. Developments are reported today on Page B5.

2005-01-26.title: Democrats assail Rice, citing role on Iraq

2005-01-27.title: Accord on Zhao Funeral Is Reported in China
2005-01-27.abstract: Chinese officials and family of late Communist Party chief Zhao Ziyang have agreed to hold public burial service for him, resolving political tensions that arose after his death; he was purged after opposing 1989 crackdown on dissent (M)

2005-01-27.title: Russia will pursue Ukraine's No. 2 in bribe case
Across Baghdad, Vote Brings Fear
2005-01-27.abstract: When American troops overthrew the government of Saddam Hussein nearly two years ago, Raad al-Naqib finally felt free. But Dr. Naqib, 46, a dentist, will not vote on Sunday, when Iraqis have their first opportunity in a generation to take part in an election with no predetermined outcome. It is far too dangerous, he said.    Some American commanders say that Baghdad is not safe even for their troops.

2005-01-27.title: Turkey Ready to Intervene
2005-01-27.abstract: Senior army general says Turkey's military is prepared to intervene if postelection clashes erupt in northern Iraq or if Iraqi Kurds try to form independent state; statement underscores Turkey's growing anxiety over Iraqi elections (S)

2005-01-27.title: France Seizes 11 Accused of Plotting Iraq Attacks
2005-01-27.abstract: French intelligence and law enforcement officials announce they have smashed cell that was recruiting fighters to join insurgency in Iraq; say they arrested nine men and two women after intensive and lengthy surveillance, and that more arrests are expected; timing of arrests is linked to information that a least two of those arrested were poised to leave for Iraq, perhaps to carry out suicide attacks; this is first such operation in France since Interior Min Dominique de Villepin stepped up surveillance of mosques, prayer halls and demonstrations throughout France; in Germany, two men are arrested on suspicion of being part of operation helping dozen men travel from Germany to Iraq to join insurgency; German officials suspect at least two of men were planning suicide attacks; photo (M)

2005-01-28.title: With Hope, Expatriate Iraqis Line Up to Vote in 14 Countries
In Bush's Words: Iraq, Abortion, Social Security, Diplomacy and the Presidency
2005-01-28.abstract: Excerpts from interview with Pres Bush, primarily about Iraq (M)

2005-01-28.title: Brazilian Leader Hears Boos From Unusual Direction, the Left
2005-01-28.abstract: Pres Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva of Brazil defends his record before thousands of skeptical antiglobalization activists attending fifth annual World Social Forum in Porto Alegre; says his government is painstakingly laying foundation to eradicate Brazil's gaping social disparities; seeks to answer growing chorus of critics on left who accuse him of abandoning his working-class roots and selling out to global capitalism (M)

2005-01-28.title: In Violence-Prone Mosul, Voters Will Need a Shield of Snipers
2005-01-28.abstract: Election will be held in violence-racked city of Mosul, but it remains to be seen if enough people will brave dangers to vote in significant numbers; Mosul is one of many Sunni Arab cities where insurgent presence is strong; even handling election materials is considered so dangerous that ballots and ballot boxes will be distributed to polling centers by armored American military convoys; scores of civilians have died in attacks and Kurds, government officials and Iraqi security officers have been massacred since Nov, when thousands of police and security officers abandoned their posts under insurgent assault that coincided with American attack on Falluja; Mosul's 700 election workers, threatened by insurgents, walked off job; new election workers were recruited with promises of free food, lodging and bonuses; American officials have been trying to convince Iraqi voters that they can vote safely, but in Mosul and other volatile cities in Anbar Provine, turnout is expected to be low; photo (M)

2005-01-28.title: Even in Paradise of Turquoise Waters, Absence and Stress Take Their Toll
2005-01-28.abstract: Death of marines in helicopter crash in sandstorm in Iraq is deeply felt at Marine Corps Base Hawaii in Kaneohe, where 26 of marines and one sailor were stationed; photo (M)

2005-01-29.title: Shiite Faction Ready to Shun Sunday's Election in Iraq
2005-01-29.abstract: Sheik Nasir Al Saedy, popular Shiite cleric, does not utter word about nationwide elections in sermon to 20,000 Iraqis less than 48 hours before vote; his silence forshadows less than overwhelming voter turnout in many parts of Iraq; Saedy is follower of rebel Shiite cleric Moktada al-Sadr, and his sermon seems to settle question of Sadr's place in country's new democratic order; Iraq's mainstream Shiite leaders want Sadr and his vast following to give up their rebellion and turn to ballot box; Sadr's silence signals that he has decided to hedge his bets against election; photo (M)

2005-01-29.title: Serb general agrees to face Hague court
A Dry Run, With Mock Voters Casting Ballots Inside, Snipers Keeping Watch Outside
2005-01-29.abstract: Polling places are being set up in Iraq under heavy security for Jan 30 elections; photo (M)

2005-01-29.title: Ex-Serbian General Agrees to Surrender to War Crimes Tribunal
2005-01-29.abstract: Retired Serbian Gen Vladimir Lazarevic agrees to surrender to UN war crimes tribunal in Hague, where he is wanted for crimes committed by his troops against ethnic Albanian civilians during 1999 war in Kosovo (M)

2005-01-29.title: Very Discreetly, China Buries Zhao, Its Troublesome Ex-Leader
2005-01-29.abstract: Some 2,000 mourners and at least that many police officers turn out for restricted funeral service for Zhao Ziyang in Beijing, as authorities take steps to ensure that final commemoration of former leader would not touch off anti-government protests (M)

2005-01-30.title: Deep Divisions Emerge in Turkey's Opposition Party
Celebration Seeps Through Diplomatic Reaction at U.N.
Iraqis Vote Amid Tight Security and Scattered Attacks
2005-01-30.abstract: Iraqis begin casting ballots for 275-member national assembly and for provincial parliaments amid scattered insurgent attacks and tight security; steady streams of people line up at checkpoints to vote; suicide bomb attack in western Baghdad kills policeman and wounds several people at checkpoint and several explosions are heard across city; voters turn out in large numbers in Kurdish areas; turn out and ease with which election is carried off are regarded as major tests of Bush administration goal of implanting democracy in Middle East; prospect of widespread Sunni boycott could mar results; map; photos (L)

2005-01-30.title: Under Pressure, Qatar May Sell Jazeera Station
2005-01-30.abstract: Bush administration has complained so heatedly to Qatari leaders that Al Jazeera's broadcasts have been inflammatory, misleading and occasionally false, especially on Iraq, that Qatar is accelerating plans to put station on market, although Bush administration may like privately owned station no better; Al Jazeera's audiences range from 30 million to 50 million, but station still relied in 2004 on $40 million to $50 million subsidy from government, out of $120 million budget (M)

2005-01-30.title: Voting, Not Violence, Is the Big Story on Arab TV

2005-01-31.title: For a Battered Populace, a Day of Civic Passion
2005-01-31.abstract: Scene at Baghdad polling station is suffused with sense of civic spirit, dispelling suspicion that 24 years of bludgeoning under Saddam Hussein had bred disabling passivity among country's 28 million people; voters walk long distances through streets choked with military checkpoints, and stand for hours in line to cast their ballots; these are same people who crowded polling centers in 2002 to re-elect Hussein in one-candidate referendum by official vote count of 100 percent; after surprise of heavy turnout on Jan 30, there is much that remains to daunt American hopes that Iraqis would come together in new political compact, one that would enable their new national assembly to draft constitution, submit it to referendum, and hold another election late this year for full-term parliament and government; Americans who have remained decisive authority in Iraq emerge from voting with large measure of vindication for having pressed ahead with election despite predictions that enterprise would collapse amid uncontrollable insurgent violence or crippling voter boycott; photos (M)

2005-01-31.title: Jury selection to begin in Michael Jackson trial
U.S. pressing Arab states on Iran's nuclear threat
White House Letter: Bush borrows a page from Natan Sharansky
